title: Indifference curves and budget lines
paperName: Cambridge International A Level Economics (P3 & P4)
summary: 9708 A Level micro — indifference curves, budget constraints, and utility maximisation with GeoGebra.
learningObjectives: Construct budget lines from income and prices.
Analyse effects of price and income changes.

simpleExplanation.title: Consumer choice theory
simpleExplanation.summary: Consumers maximise utility subject to a budget constraint. Optimal choice is where the indifference curve is tangent to the budget line.
steps: IC: higher = more utility; convex — diminishing MRS.
Price fall: budget pivots outward — substitution and income effects.

flashcards.back: A curve showing all combinations of two goods that give the consumer the same level of utility — higher curves mean higher utility.

flashcards.front: Why are indifference curves convex to the origin?
flashcards.back: Diminishing marginal rate of substitution — as you give up more of good Y, you need increasingly more of good X to maintain the same utility.
Income = P_x·X + P_y·Y. Slope = −P_x/P_y (opportunity cost of good X in terms of Y).
MRS = P_x/P_y, equivalently MU_x/P_x = MU_y/P_y — marginal rate of substitution equals the price ratio.

flashcards.front: Effect of a fall in P_x?
flashcards.back: Budget line pivots outward on the X-axis. Consumer moves to a higher indifference curve — substitution effect (more X) plus income effect (real income rises).
No — crossing would violate the assumption that higher curves represent higher utility (transitivity of preferences).

sections.content: **Indifference curves** (7.2) underpin **A Level micro essays**. Label axes, draw **higher ICs**, and show **tangency** — not just prose. This topic extends **7.1 utility** into graphical consumer theory.

An **indifference curve (IC)** shows combinations of goods X and Y giving **equal utility**. Key properties:

1. **Downward sloping** — to keep utility constant, more X requires less Y.
2. **Convex to origin** — diminishing **marginal rate of substitution (MRS)**.
3. **Higher IC = higher utility** — consumer prefers bundles on higher curves.
4. **Never cross** — each point has one utility level.

The **budget line** shows all affordable bundles given income (Y) and prices:

**Y = P_x·X + P_y·Y**

**Slope = −P_x/P_y** — the opportunity cost of one more unit of X.

**X-intercept** = Y/P_x; **Y-intercept** = Y/P_y.

A **fall in P_x** pivots the line outward on the X-axis (same Y-intercept). A **rise in income** shifts the entire line outward parallel.

sections.question:
A consumer has income of £100. Good X costs £5 and Good Y costs £10.

(a) Write the budget equation and find the intercepts.
(b) At the optimum, the consumer buys 8 units of X and 6 units of Y. Verify this is on the budget line and state the MRS at the optimum.
sections.solution:
(a) **Budget equation:** £100 = 5X + 10Y

**X-intercept:** 100/5 = **20 units of X**
**Y-intercept:** 100/10 = **10 units of Y**
**Slope:** −5/10 = **−0.5** (1 X costs 0.5 Y)

(b) **Check bundle (8, 6):**
5(8) + 10(6) = 40 + 60 = **£100** ✓ — on the budget line.

**MRS at optimum = P_x/P_y = 5/10 = 0.5**

The consumer is willing to trade 0.5 units of Y for 1 unit of X (at the margin), which equals the market price ratio.

sections.content:
When **P_x falls**, the budget line **pivots** outward. The consumer reaches a **higher IC** at a new tangency — buys more X.

This decomposes into:
- **Substitution effect** — X relatively cheaper → buy more X, less Y.
- **Income effect** — real purchasing power rises → buy more of normal goods.

For a **Giffen good** (rare), the income effect dominates and demand curve slopes upward.

Always draw **three elements** on one diagram: at least two ICs (label U₁, U₂), the budget line(s), and the tangency point. Use a **pivot** for price changes and a **parallel shift** for income changes.

steps.caption: Indifference curve: combinations giving equal utility — convex to origin.
Budget line: income = P_x·X + P_y·Y — slope −P_x/P_y.
Utility max at tangency: MRS = P_x/P_y.
Income/substitution effects from price change (extension).
steps.embedHint: Decompose movement along new budget line.
